role_summary: Civil rights movement organizer; in this corpus's embedding his testimony pairs strongly with Charles McLaurin and clusters across SNCC, SCLC, and West Coast civil rights cohorts.
ai_reading: Tuttle's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 222), with Annie Pearl Avery (entry 3), Steven McNichols (entry 118), Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26), and C. T. Vivian (entry 134) rounding out the top five. The cluster spans the Mississippi SNCC voter-registration cohort (McLaurin, Avery), the Memphis civil-rights-legal node (Bailey), and the SCLC direct-action wing (Vivian); the embedding reads Tuttle as a cross-network movement voice broader than a single-region label. His axis positions are all in a moderate range (-0.34 to +0.39), placing him among the catalog's centrist voices without the extreme readings other entries surface; the embedding's strongest reading of him is his connectivity to McLaurin's Mississippi work, the catalog's most-cited single neighbor in the audited subset.
biographical_paragraph: Rick Tuttle's oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 105 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing is pending; the strongest signal currently available for movement role is the embedding pattern visible in the AI's reading above.
movement_context: Tuttle was active in 1963 across two of the geographically distinct organizing fronts the embedding's neighbor cluster maps onto: the SNCC Mississippi Delta voter-registration project running out of Greenwood, and the SCLC-affiliated direct-action and voter-registration work in Savannah, Georgia [src: 2, 3]. The Greenwood project had been initiated by SNCC field secretary Sam Block in June 1962, when he was sent into Leflore County by Bob Moses and Amzie Moore to establish SNCC's first Mississippi Delta voter-registration field office [src: 4]. By 1963 the Greenwood work was running under the umbrella of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO), the coalition of SNCC, CORE, NAACP, and SCLC Mississippi branches that pooled the four organizations' field-staff resources [src: 5]. Willie Peacock, the project organizer under whose direction Tuttle worked, was a Tallahatchie County native recruited into SNCC after his Holly Springs organizing work with Frank Smith; on March 6, 1963 a SNCC vehicle carrying Peacock and three companions was riddled with 27 bullets in an attack later traced to a local police officer [src: 6]. The Council's Greenwood field staff conducted door-to-door canvassing under the surveillance of the White Citizens Council, county sheriff, and registrar Martha Lamb's literacy-test apparatus, and supported activists detained in the Winona jail after the June 9, 1963 Winona bus-station arrest of Fannie Lou Hamer, Annell Ponder, and others [src: 4]. Driven out of Mississippi by threats, Tuttle relocated to Savannah, where the Chatham County Crusade for Voters, founded by Hosea Williams and his organizing base of working-class Black Savannians, had broken with the NAACP in 1962 and built a mass-mobilization model that turned out approximately 8,000 Black Savannians for the summer 1963 direct-action campaign [src: 7]. Williams himself was held on cascading bonds escalating from $200 to roughly $70,000 across eighteen days that summer; mass arrests packed the city's jail cells of ten-person capacity with seventy-five demonstrators each [src: 7]. The 1961 Freedom Rides Tuttle had participated in two years earlier had been a CORE-initiated project taken up by SNCC's Nashville cohort after Anniston and Birmingham mob attacks halted the original ride; the Mississippi arrests of Riders at the Jackson bus terminal that May produced the legal pressure on the Interstate Commerce Commission that yielded the November 1, 1961 desegregation order [src: 8].
legacy_and_reception: Tuttle's post-movement career sits in a documentary record separate from his civil rights work but parallel to the same Los Angeles civic infrastructure many of the catalog's West Coast voices interface with. He completed his master's degree at UCLA in 1964, finished his doctorate there in 1975, and served as associate dean of student activities at UCLA before entering elected office [src: 9]. He served eight years on the Los Angeles Community College Board of Trustees, including a term as board president in 1982-1983, then was elected to four four-year terms as Los Angeles City Controller, holding that office from 1985 to 2001 [src: 9, 10]. After the statutory term limit closed his City Controller tenure, he returned to UCLA as executive director of the Dashew International Center for Students and Scholars through 2006, then continued as a lecturer in the UCLA Luskin School of Public Affairs, where he was voted public policy professor of the year three times [src: 9]. His civic recognitions include the Equal Justice in Government Award from the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund and the Distinguished Public Service Award from the Pacific Southwest Region of the Anti-Defamation League [src: 9]. The 2013 oral history David P. Cline conducted with him for the Civil Rights History Project, recorded in Culver City on April 11, 2013, is the primary first-person archival record of his Greenwood and Savannah years [src: 1]. The interview also figures in the parallel Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture collection of Civil Rights History Project interviews (NMAAHC accession 2011.174.78), where it is catalogued alongside the other AFC 2010/039 interviews produced through the Library of Congress and Smithsonian collaboration [src: 11].

gallery.alt: Rick Tuttle, Civil Rights History Project oral history portrait, Culver City, California, April 11, 2013
gallery.caption: Rick Tuttle photographed during the Library of Congress Civil Rights History Project oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Culver City, California on April 11, 2013. The Civil Rights History Project (AFC 2010/039) is the joint Library of Congress and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture collection authorized by the Civil Rights History Project Act of 2009.

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854; the standard institutional history of SNCC organizing in the Mississippi Delta including Greenwood, Leflore County

sources.title: Rolundus R. Rice, Hosea Williams: A Lifetime of Defiance and Protest
sources.publisher: University of South Carolina Press, 2021; WorldCat title-detail 1156437886; the first comprehensive scholarly biography of Williams, including the Chatham County Crusade for Voters and 1963 Savannah campaign

sources.title: Raymond Arsenault, Freedom Riders: 1961 and the Struggle for Racial Justice
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 2006; WorldCat title-detail 61703944; the standard scholarly account of the 1961 Freedom Rides and recipient of the Owsley Prize from the Southern Historical Association
